subject: HTML, CSS and JavaScript
topics: CSS Flexbox — flex containers, justify-content, align-items
CSS Grid layout — grid-column, grid-row, grid positioning
Advanced CSS positioning — fixed, absolute, relative
Circular images with border-radius, viewport heights
Fixed sidebar navigation with flex layouts

questions.question: Which CSS property is used to specify the font family for text?
options: font-style
font-size
font-family
text-decoration
questions.explanation: The `font-family` property defines the font for text. It accepts a list of font names as fallbacks.
questions.hint: Think about which property directly controls the typeface used for text.

questions.question: What is the primary benefit of using Google Fonts?
options: They are pre-installed on all operating systems.
They offer a wide variety of free, web-optimized fonts.
They automatically improve website SEO.
They reduce website loading times.
questions.explanation: Google Fonts provides a large library of free, open-source fonts optimized for web use, improving design flexibility.
questions.hint: Consider the accessibility and variety of fonts available online.

questions.question: Which color code represents a shade of red with maximum intensity?
options: #FFFF00
questions.explanation: #FF0000 represents pure red in the hexadecimal color model, with maximum red and no green or blue.
questions.hint: Remember the order of RGB in hexadecimal color codes.

questions.question: What is the difference between RGB and HSL color models?
options: RGB defines colors based on hue, saturation, and lightness, while HSL uses red, green, and blue.
HSL defines colors based on hue, saturation, and lightness, while RGB uses red, green, and blue.
RGB is only for print, while HSL is for web.
There is no difference; they are interchangeable.
questions.explanation: HSL (Hue, Saturation, Lightness) provides a more intuitive way to define colors based on perceptual qualities, while RGB uses additive color mixing.
questions.hint: Think about how humans perceive color – is it more natural to think in terms of shades and tones or primary colors?

questions.question: Which CSS property creates a smooth transition between two states?
options: animation
transform
transition
keyframes
questions.explanation: The `transition` property allows you to specify how property changes should be animated over a given duration.
questions.hint: This property is used to create visual effects when properties change.

questions.question: What does `justify-content: space-between;` do in a flex container?
options: Distributes items evenly with equal space around them.
Distributes items evenly with space at the beginning and end.
Distributes items evenly with space between each item.
Aligns items to the start of the container.
questions.explanation: `justify-content: space-between;` distributes space evenly between flex items, with the first and last items aligned to the container's edges.
questions.hint: Consider how the space is allocated among the flex items.

questions.question: Which property controls the vertical alignment of items within a flex container?
options: justify-content
align-items
flex-direction
flex-wrap
questions.explanation: `align-items` controls the vertical alignment of flex items along the cross axis.
questions.hint: Think about which axis controls vertical positioning in a flex container.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a card-based design?
options: To create a linear, scrolling layout.
To present content in visually distinct, self-contained blocks.
To maximize screen real estate.
To simplify website navigation.
questions.explanation: Card-based designs organize content into visually separate cards, improving readability and user experience.
questions.hint: Consider how information is grouped and presented in modern web designs.

questions.question: How can you create a hover effect on a card that changes its background color?
options: Using the `:hover` pseudo-class and the `background-color` property.
Using JavaScript event listeners.
Using the `animation` property.
Using the `transform` property.
questions.explanation: The `:hover` pseudo-class allows you to apply styles when the mouse cursor is over an element, and `background-color` changes the background.
questions.hint: Think about CSS pseudo-classes that respond to user interactions.

questions.question: What is the main difference between CSS Flexbox and CSS Grid?
options: Flexbox is for one-dimensional layouts, while Grid is for two-dimensional layouts.
Grid is for one-dimensional layouts, while Flexbox is for two-dimensional layouts.
Flexbox is only for aligning items, while Grid is for creating entire page layouts.
There is no significant difference between them.
questions.explanation: Flexbox excels at arranging items in a single row or column, while Grid provides control over both rows and columns for complex layouts.
questions.hint: Consider the dimensionality of the layouts each system is designed for.

questions.question: In CSS Grid, what do `grid-column-start` and `grid-column-end` define?
options: The number of rows an item spans.
The starting and ending grid lines for a column.
The alignment of an item within a grid cell.
The gap between grid items.
questions.explanation: `grid-column-start` and `grid-column-end` specify the grid lines where a grid item begins and ends in the column direction.
questions.hint: Think about how grid lines define the structure of a grid layout.

questions.question: What does the `object-fit` property control?
options: The size of the container.
How an image or video should be resized to fit its container.
The alignment of text within an element.
The spacing between grid items.
questions.explanation: `object-fit` determines how the content of an element (like an image) should be resized to fit its container, options include 'cover', 'contain', 'fill', etc.
questions.hint: Consider how images are scaled and cropped within their containers.

questions.question: Which `object-fit` value ensures the entire image is visible within its container, potentially leaving empty space?
options: cover
contain
fill
none
questions.explanation: `object-fit: contain;` scales the image to fit within the container while maintaining its aspect ratio, potentially leaving empty space.
questions.hint: Think about which value prioritizes showing the entire image.

questions.question: What is the purpose of using `viewport` units (e.g., `vh`, `vw`)?
options: To specify sizes relative to the root element.
To specify sizes relative to the viewport.
To specify sizes in pixels.
To specify sizes in ems.
questions.explanation: Viewport units (`vh` and `vw`) are relative to the viewport's height and width, making them useful for responsive design.
questions.hint: Consider how these units relate to the browser window size.

questions.question: How can you create a fixed sidebar navigation that remains visible while scrolling?
options: Using `position: relative;`.
Using `position: absolute;`.
Using `position: fixed;`.
Using `position: static;`.
questions.explanation: `position: fixed;` removes the element from the normal document flow and positions it relative to the viewport, keeping it fixed during scrolling.
questions.hint: Think about which positioning value keeps an element in a constant location on the screen.

questions.question: What is the difference between `position: relative;` and `position: static;`?
options: `relative` positions the element relative to its normal position, while `static` is the default positioning.
`static` positions the element relative to its normal position, while `relative` is the default positioning.
They are identical.
`relative` is used for fixed positioning, while `static` is for absolute positioning.
questions.explanation: `position: static;` is the default, meaning the element is positioned according to the normal document flow. `position: relative;` allows you to offset the element from its normal position.
questions.hint: Consider how each positioning value affects the element's placement in the document.

questions.question: How can you create a circular image using CSS?
options: Using the `shape-outside` property.
Using the `border-radius: 50%;` property.
Using the `clip-path` property.
Using the `transform: rotate(360deg);` property.
questions.explanation: Setting `border-radius: 50%;` on an image with equal width and height will create a circular shape.
questions.hint: Think about which property controls the rounding of corners.

questions.question: What is the purpose of CSS gradients?
options: To add shadows to elements.
To create smooth transitions between colors.
To define the background color of an element.
To animate elements on the page.
questions.explanation: CSS gradients create a smooth transition between two or more colors, adding visual interest to backgrounds and other elements.
questions.hint: Consider how gradients create a visual effect of blending colors.

questions.question: Which type of gradient creates a color transition from a central point?
options: Linear gradient
Radial gradient
Conic gradient
Repeating gradient
questions.explanation: A radial gradient transitions colors from a central point, creating a circular or elliptical effect.
questions.hint: Think about the shape of the color transition.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the `conic-gradient()` function in CSS?
options: To create a gradient that transitions colors along a line.
To create a gradient that transitions colors around a center point in a circular fashion.
To create a repeating linear gradient.
To create a gradient with sharp color boundaries.
questions.explanation: The `conic-gradient()` function creates a gradient that transitions colors around a center point, similar to a color wheel.
questions.hint: Consider the shape and direction of the color transition.

questions.question: What is the effect of `transition: all 0.3s ease;`?
options: Applies a 0.3-second transition to all properties with an ease timing function.
Applies a 0.3-second transition to only the background color.
Applies a 0.3-second transition to only the width and height.
Disables all transitions.
questions.explanation: This CSS rule applies a smooth 0.3-second transition to all animatable properties, using the 'ease' timing function for a natural acceleration/deceleration effect.
questions.hint: Think about what 'all' means in the context of CSS transitions.

questions.question: Given the following CSS: `.card { width: 200px; height: 300px; overflow: hidden; } .card img { width: 100%; height: auto; }`, what will happen to an image inside the card if the image's aspect ratio doesn't match the card's?
options: The image will be stretched to fit the card.
The image will be cropped to fit the card.
The image will overflow the card.
The image will be scaled down to fit within the card while maintaining its aspect ratio.
questions.explanation: The `overflow: hidden;` property on the card will hide any part of the image that extends beyond the card's boundaries, and `height: auto;` maintains the aspect ratio.
questions.hint: Consider the interaction between `overflow: hidden;` and `height: auto;`.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the `align-content` property in Flexbox?
options: To align items along the main axis.
To align items along the cross axis.
To distribute space between lines of flex items.
To control the order of flex items.
questions.explanation: `align-content` controls how flex lines are distributed along the cross axis when there is extra space in the flex container.
questions.hint: Think about how this property affects multiple lines of flex items.

questions.question: Which CSS property is used to add space between grid items?
options: margin
padding
grid-gap
border-spacing
questions.explanation: `grid-gap` (or `grid-row-gap` and `grid-column-gap`) is used to specify the space between grid items.
questions.hint: Consider properties specifically designed for grid layouts.

questions.question: What is the difference between `em` and `rem` units in CSS?
options: `em` is relative to the root element, while `rem` is relative to the parent element.
`rem` is relative to the root element, while `em` is relative to the parent element.
They are identical.
`em` is used for font sizes, while `rem` is used for margins and padding.
questions.explanation: `rem` (root em) is relative to the font size of the root element (`<html>`), providing consistent scaling, while `em` is relative to the font size of the parent element.
questions.hint: Think about the base element each unit references.

questions.question: What does `z-index` property control?
options: The size of an element.
The stacking order of elements.
The transparency of an element.
The animation speed of an element.
questions.explanation: The `z-index` property specifies the stack order of an element, controlling which elements appear in front of others.
questions.hint: Consider how elements overlap on a webpage.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the `::before` and `::after` pseudo-elements?
options: To style the first and last child elements.
To insert content before or after an element's content.
To create hover effects.
To add animations to elements.
questions.explanation: The `::before` and `::after` pseudo-elements allow you to insert content (text, images, etc.) before or after the content of an element.
questions.hint: Think about how you can add content without modifying the HTML.

questions.question: Which CSS property is used to make an element transparent?
options: color
background-color
opacity
visibility
questions.explanation: The `opacity` property controls the transparency of an element, with values ranging from 0 (fully transparent) to 1 (fully opaque).
questions.hint: Consider which property affects the overall visibility of an element.

questions.question: What is the effect of `display: none;`?
options: Hides the element but still takes up space in the layout.
Completely removes the element from the document flow.
Makes the element transparent.
Disables the element's functionality.
questions.explanation: `display: none;` removes the element from the document flow, meaning it doesn't take up any space and is not rendered.
questions.hint: Think about how this property affects the layout of the page.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the `box-sizing` property?
options: To control the size of the box model.
To control the spacing between elements.
To control the alignment of elements.
To control the color of the box model.
questions.explanation: The `box-sizing` property determines how the total width and height of an element are calculated, with `border-box` including padding and border in the specified dimensions.
questions.hint: Consider how padding and border affect the overall size of an element.

questions.question: What is the primary advantage of using CSS variables (custom properties)?
options: They improve website security.
They allow for easier theme switching and code maintainability.
They reduce website loading times.
They automatically optimize images.
questions.explanation: CSS variables allow you to define reusable values that can be easily updated throughout your stylesheet, simplifying theme changes and code maintenance.
questions.hint: Think about how you can make your CSS more flexible and reusable.

questions.question: Given the following CSS: `.container { display: grid; grid-template-columns: repeat(3, 1fr); }`, what does this code do?
options: Creates a flex container with three equal-width items.
Creates a grid container with three equal-width columns.
Creates a container with a fixed width of 300px.
Creates a container with three rows.
questions.explanation: This code creates a grid container with three columns, each taking up an equal fraction (1fr) of the available space.
questions.hint: Consider the meaning of `grid-template-columns` and `repeat()`.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the `clamp()` function in CSS?
options: To create a gradient.
To limit a value within a specified range.
To animate an element.
To change the font size.
questions.explanation: The `clamp()` function limits a value between a minimum, preferred, and maximum value, useful for responsive typography and sizing.
questions.hint: Think about how you can ensure a value stays within certain bounds.

questions.question: What is the difference between `visibility: hidden;` and `display: none;`?
options: `visibility: hidden;` removes the element from the document flow, while `display: none;` hides it but keeps its space.
`display: none;` removes the element from the document flow, while `visibility: hidden;` hides it but keeps its space.
They are identical.
Neither property hides the element.
questions.explanation: `display: none;` removes the element from the document flow, while `visibility: hidden;` hides the element but it still occupies space in the layout.
questions.hint: Consider how each property affects the layout of the page.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the `filter` property in CSS?
options: To change the color of an element.
To apply visual effects like blur, grayscale, and contrast.
To add a shadow to an element.
To animate an element.
questions.explanation: The `filter` property allows you to apply various visual effects to an element, such as blur, grayscale, contrast, and more.
questions.hint: Think about how you can modify the appearance of an element without changing its content.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the `backdrop-filter` property in CSS?
options: To filter the element itself.
To filter the content behind the element.
To add a shadow to the element.
To animate the element.
questions.explanation: The `backdrop-filter` property applies visual effects to the area behind an element, creating a blurred or frosted glass effect.
questions.hint: Consider how this property affects the background of an element.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the `will-change` property in CSS?
options: To change the color of an element.
To inform the browser about upcoming changes to an element.
To add a shadow to an element.
To animate an element.
questions.explanation: The `will-change` property informs the browser about upcoming changes to an element, allowing it to optimize performance.
questions.hint: Think about how you can improve the performance of animations and transitions.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the `content` property in CSS when used with pseudo-elements?
options: To specify the color of the pseudo-element.
To insert text or other content into the pseudo-element.
To control the size of the pseudo-element.
To animate the pseudo-element.
questions.explanation: The `content` property is used to insert text, images, or other content into pseudo-elements like `::before` and `::after`.
questions.hint: Think about how you can add content without modifying the HTML.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the `caret-color` property in CSS?
options: To change the color of the text.
To change the color of the cursor.
To change the color of the text selection caret.
To animate the text.
questions.explanation: The `caret-color` property specifies the color of the text selection caret (the blinking cursor).
questions.hint: Consider which part of the text is affected by this property.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the `hyphens` property in CSS?
options: To control the alignment of text.
To control the spacing between letters.
To control how words are broken at the end of a line.
To change the font size.
questions.explanation: The `hyphens` property controls how words are broken at the end of a line, improving readability in justified text.
questions.hint: Think about how words are split when they reach the end of a line.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the `writing-mode` property in CSS?
options: To control the alignment of text.
To control the direction of text.
To control the spacing between letters.
To change the font size.
questions.explanation: The `writing-mode` property specifies the direction of text, allowing for vertical or horizontal writing.
questions.hint: Consider how text can be displayed in different orientations.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the `text-wrap: balance;` property in CSS?
options: To wrap text evenly across multiple lines.
To balance the last line of text in a paragraph.
To prevent text from wrapping.
To control the spacing between words.
questions.explanation: The `text-wrap: balance;` property attempts to distribute text evenly across multiple lines, improving visual balance.
questions.hint: Think about how this property affects the appearance of paragraphs.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the `shape-outside` property in CSS?
options: To change the shape of an element.
To define the shape around which text will wrap.
To add a shadow to an element.
To animate an element.
questions.explanation: The `shape-outside` property defines a shape around which text will wrap, creating interesting visual effects.
questions.hint: Consider how text can flow around an element with a custom shape.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the `mask-image` property in CSS?
options: To change the color of an element.
To use an image to mask an element's transparency.
To add a shadow to an element.
To animate an element.
questions.explanation: The `mask-image` property uses an image to define the transparency of an element, creating complex shapes and effects.
questions.hint: Think about how you can use an image to control the visibility of an element.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the `scroll-snap-type` property in CSS?
options: To control the scrolling speed.
To create a snapping effect when scrolling.
To add a shadow to the scrollbar.
To animate the scrollbar.
questions.explanation: The `scroll-snap-type` property creates a snapping effect when scrolling, making it easier to navigate to specific sections.
questions.hint: Consider how this property affects the user's scrolling experience.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the `initial-letter` property in CSS?
options: To change the color of the first letter.
To create a drop cap effect for the first letter of a paragraph.
To add a shadow to the first letter.
To animate the first letter.
questions.explanation: The `initial-letter` property creates a drop cap effect, making the first letter of a paragraph larger and more prominent.
questions.hint: Think about how you can visually emphasize the beginning of a paragraph.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the `text-emphasis-color` property in CSS?
options: To change the color of the text.
To change the color of the text selection.
To highlight specific characters in the text.
To add a shadow to the text.
questions.explanation: The `text-emphasis-color` property highlights specific characters in the text, such as punctuation marks or emphasis markers.
questions.hint: Consider how you can draw attention to specific parts of the text.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the `line-clamp` property in CSS?
options: To control the line height.
To limit the number of lines of text displayed.
To control the spacing between lines.
To change the font size.
questions.explanation: The `line-clamp` property limits the number of lines of text displayed, adding an ellipsis (...) to indicate truncated content.
questions.hint: Think about how you can truncate long text blocks.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the `contain: layout;` property in CSS?
options: To contain the overflow of an element.
To isolate an element's layout from the rest of the page.
To add a shadow to an element.
To animate an element.
questions.explanation: The `contain: layout;` property isolates an element's layout from the rest of the page, improving performance by preventing unnecessary recalculations.
questions.hint: Consider how you can optimize rendering performance.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the `accent-color` property in CSS?
options: To change the color of the text.
To change the color of form controls.
To add a shadow to an element.
To animate an element.
questions.explanation: The `accent-color` property allows you to customize the color of form controls, such as checkboxes, radio buttons, and sliders.
questions.hint: Consider how you can style form elements to match your website's design.
